Romanian armament during World War II
first_paragraph: The [Kingdom of Romania](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Romania "Kingdom of Romania"), under the rule of [King Carol II](/wiki/King_Carol_II_of_Romania "King Carol II of Romania"), initially maintained [neutrality](/wiki/Neutral_country "Neutral country") in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). However, [fascist](/wiki/Fascist "Fascist") political forces, especially the [Iron Guard](/wiki/Iron_Guard "Iron Guard"), rose in popularity and power, urging an alliance with [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") and its [allies](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers"). As the military fortunes of Romania's two main guarantors of territorial integrity—[France](/wiki/French_Third_Republic "French Third Republic") and [Britain](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom")—crumbled in the [Battle of France](/wiki/Battle_of_France "Battle of France"), the government of Romania turned to Germany in hopes of a similar guarantee, unaware that Germany, in [the supplementary protocol](/wiki/Molotov%E2%80%93Ribbentrop_Pact#Secret_protocol "Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact") to the 1939 [Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact](/wiki/Molotov%E2%80%93Ribbentrop_Pact "Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact"), had already granted its permission to [Soviet](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") claims on [Romanian territory](/wiki/Bessarabia "Bessarabia").

The [Kingdom of Romania](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Romania "Kingdom of Romania"), under the rule of [King Carol II](/wiki/King_Carol_II_of_Romania "King Carol II of Romania"), initially maintained [neutrality](/wiki/Neutral_country "Neutral country") in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). However, [fascist](/wiki/Fascist "Fascist") political forces, especially the [Iron Guard](/wiki/Iron_Guard "Iron Guard"), rose in popularity and power, urging an alliance with [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") and its [allies](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers"). As the military fortunes of Romania's two main guarantors of territorial integrity—[France](/wiki/French_Third_Republic "French Third Republic") and [Britain](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom")—crumbled in the [Battle of France](/wiki/Battle_of_France "Battle of France"), the government of Romania turned to Germany in hopes of a similar guarantee, unaware that Germany, in [the supplementary protocol](/wiki/Molotov%E2%80%93Ribbentrop_Pact#Secret_protocol "Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact") to the 1939 [Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact](/wiki/Molotov%E2%80%93Ribbentrop_Pact "Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact"), had already granted its permission to [Soviet](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") claims on [Romanian territory](/wiki/Bessarabia "Bessarabia").

In the summer of 1940, the [USSR](/wiki/USSR "USSR") [occupied Bessarabia and Northern Bukovina](/wiki/Soviet_occupation_of_Bessarabia_and_Northern_Bukovina "Soviet occupation of Bessarabia and Northern Bukovina"), severely weakening Romania and diminishing its international standing. Taking advantage of the situation, [Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary_(1920%E2%80%931946) "Kingdom of Hungary (1920–1946)") and [Bulgaria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Bulgaria "Kingdom of Bulgaria") both pressed territorial claims on Romania. The disputes, arbitrated by [Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") and [Italy](/wiki/Fascist_Italy "Fascist Italy"), led to the further losses of [Northern Transylvania](/wiki/Northern_Transylvania "Northern Transylvania") and [Southern Dobruja](/wiki/Southern_Dobruja "Southern Dobruja") to [Hungary](/wiki/Second_Vienna_Award "Second Vienna Award") and [Bulgaria](/wiki/Treaty_of_Craiova "Treaty of Craiova") respectively. The popularity of the Romanian government plummeted, further reinforcing fascist and military factions, who eventually staged a coup in September 1940 that turned the country into a [Legionary State](/wiki/National_Legionary_State "National Legionary State") under [Mareșal](/wiki/Mare%C8%99al_(Romania) "Mareșal (Romania)") [Ion Antonescu](/wiki/Ion_Antonescu "Ion Antonescu") in partnership with the Iron Guard. Romania officially joined the [Axis powers](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers") on 23 November 1940. Antonescu [assumed full control](/wiki/Legionnaires%27_rebellion_and_Bucharest_pogrom "Legionnaires' rebellion and Bucharest pogrom") over Romania in January 1941 and [invaded the Soviet Union](/wiki/Invaded_the_Soviet_Union "Invaded the Soviet Union") with the [Axis](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers"), providing equipment and [oil](/wiki/Petrochemical_industry_in_Romania "Petrochemical industry in Romania") to Germany and committing more troops to the [Eastern Front](/wiki/Eastern_Front_(World_War_II) "Eastern Front (World War II)") than all other allies of Germany combined. Romanian forces played a large role during fighting in [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine#Ukraine_during_World_War_II "Ukraine"), [Bessarabia](/wiki/Bessarabia "Bessarabia"), and in the [Battle of Stalingrad](/wiki/Battle_of_Stalingrad "Battle of Stalingrad"). Romanian troops were responsible for the [persecution and massacre](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Romania#The_Holocaust "History of the Jews in Romania") of 260,000 Jews in Romanian-controlled territories, though half of the Jews living in Romania survived the war.[[1]](#cite_note-Study-1) Romania controlled the third-largest Axis army in Europe and the fourth largest Axis army in the world.

[The Allies bombed Romania](/wiki/Bombing_of_Romania_in_World_War_II "Bombing of Romania in World War II") heavily from 1943 onwards, and during the [Battle of Romania](/wiki/Battle_of_Romania "Battle of Romania"), Soviet armies [occupied](/wiki/Soviet_occupation_of_Romania "Soviet occupation of Romania") the country in 1944. Popular support for Romania's participation in the war faltered, and the German–Romanian fronts collapsed under the Soviet onslaught. King [Michael of Romania](/wiki/Michael_of_Romania "Michael of Romania") engineered the [1944 Romanian coup d'état](/wiki/1944_Romanian_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1944 Romanian coup d'état") that deposed the Antonescu regime and put Romania on the side of the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II") for the remainder of the war. Despite this late association with the victors, [Greater Romania](/wiki/Greater_Romania "Greater Romania") was not restored, except for [Northern Transylvania](/wiki/Northern_Transylvania "Northern Transylvania") from Hungary.

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_in_World_War_II&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

See also: [Little Entente](/wiki/Little_Entente "Little Entente"), [Polish–Romanian alliance](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Romanian_alliance "Polish–Romanian alliance"), [Second Vienna Award](/wiki/Second_Vienna_Award "Second Vienna Award"), and [Soviet occupation of Bessarabia and northern Bukovina](/wiki/Soviet_occupation_of_Bessarabia_and_northern_Bukovina "Soviet occupation of Bessarabia and northern Bukovina")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/Romania_1930_ethnic_map_EN.png/250px-Romania_1930_ethnic_map_EN.png)](/wiki/File:Romania_1930_ethnic_map_EN.png)

Ethnic map of Greater Romania according to the [1930 Romanian census](/w/index.php?title=1930_Romanian_census&action=edit&redlink=1 "1930 Romanian census (page does not exist)"). Sizeable ethnic minorities put Romania at odds with Hungary, Bulgaria, and the Soviet Union throughout the interwar period.

In the aftermath of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), Romania, which fought alongside the [Entente powers](/wiki/Triple_Entente "Triple Entente") against the [Central Powers](/wiki/Central_Powers "Central Powers"), had greatly expanded its territory, incorporating the regions of [Transylvania](/wiki/Transylvania "Transylvania"), [Bessarabia](/wiki/Bessarabia "Bessarabia") and [Bukovina](/wiki/Bukovina "Bukovina"), largely as a result of the vacuum created by the collapse of the [Austro-Hungarian](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_Empire "Austro-Hungarian Empire") and [Russian](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") empires. This led to the achievement of the long-standing nationalist goal of creating a "[Greater Romania](/wiki/Greater_Romania "Greater Romania")", a national state that would incorporate all ethnic Romanians. However, the newly gained territories also included significant Hungarian, German, Bulgarian, Ukrainian, and Russian minorities, which put Romania at odds with several of her neighbours.[[2]](#cite_note-2) This occasionally led to violent conflicts, as exemplified by the [Hungarian–Romanian War](/wiki/Hungarian%E2%80%93Romanian_War "Hungarian–Romanian War") and the [Tatarbunary Uprising](/wiki/Tatarbunary_Uprising "Tatarbunary Uprising"). To contain Hungarian irredentism, Romania, [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Yugoslavia "Yugoslavia") and [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia") established the [Little Entente](/wiki/Little_Entente "Little Entente") in 1921. That same year Romania and Poland concluded a [defensive alliance](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Romanian_alliance "Polish–Romanian alliance") against the emergent Soviet Union, and in 1934 the [Balkan Entente](/wiki/Balkan_Pact "Balkan Pact") was formed with Yugoslavia, Greece and Turkey, which were suspicious of Bulgaria.[[3]](#cite_note-Axworthy,_p._13-3)

Since the late 19th century onwards Romania had been a relatively democratic [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy") with a pro-Western outlook, but the country faced increasing turmoil in the 1930s as a result of the [Great Depression in Romania](/wiki/Great_Depression_in_Romania "Great Depression in Romania") and the rise of fascist movements such as the [Iron Guard](/wiki/Iron_Guard "Iron Guard"), which advocated revolutionary terrorism against the state. Under the pretext of stabilizing the country, the increasingly autocratic King [Carol II](/wiki/Carol_II "Carol II") proclaimed a 'royal dictatorship' in 1938. The new regime featured [corporatist](/wiki/Corporatist "Corporatist") policies that often resembled those of Fascist Italy and Nazi Germany.[[4]](#cite_note-4) In parallel with these internal developments, economic pressures and a weak Franco-British response to Hitler's aggressive foreign policy caused Romania to start drifting away from the Western Allies and closer to the Axis.[[3]](#cite_note-Axworthy,_p._13-3)

On 13 April 1939, France and the United Kingdom had pledged to guarantee the independence of the Kingdom of Romania. Negotiations with the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") concerning a similar guarantee collapsed when Romania refused to allow the [Red Army](/wiki/Red_Army "Red Army") to cross its frontiers.[[1]](#cite_note-Study-1)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

On 23 August 1939, Germany and the Soviet Union signed the [Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact](/wiki/Molotov%E2%80%93Ribbentrop_Pact "Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact"). Among other things, this recognized in a secret annex the Soviet "interest" in [Bessarabia](/wiki/Bessarabia "Bessarabia") (which had been ruled by the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") from 1812 to 1918). This Soviet interest was combined with a clear indication that there was an explicit lack of any German interest in the area.

Eight days later Nazi Germany invaded the [Second Polish Republic](/wiki/Second_Polish_Republic "Second Polish Republic"). Expecting military aid from Britain and France, Poland chose not to execute its alliance with Romania in order to be able to use the [Romanian Bridgehead](/wiki/Romanian_Bridgehead "Romanian Bridgehead"). Romania officially remained neutral and, under pressure from the Soviet Union and Germany, interned the fleeing Polish government after its members had crossed the Polish–Romanian border on 17 September, forcing them to relegate their authority to what became the [Polish government-in-exile](/wiki/Polish_government-in-exile "Polish government-in-exile").[[6]](#cite_note-6) After the assassination of Romanian Prime Minister [Armand Călinescu](/wiki/Armand_C%C4%83linescu "Armand Călinescu") on 21 September, King Carol II tried to maintain neutrality for several months longer, but the surrender of the [Third French Republic](/wiki/Third_French_Republic "Third French Republic") and the retreat of British forces from continental Europe rendered the assurances that both countries had made to Romania meaningless.[[1]](#cite_note-Study-1)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/P%C3%A9rdidasTerritorialesRumanas1940-ro.svg/250px-P%C3%A9rdidasTerritorialesRumanas1940-ro.svg.png)](/wiki/File:P%C3%A9rdidasTerritorialesRumanas1940-ro.svg)

Romania after the territorial losses of 1940. The recovery of Bessarabia and Northern Bukovina was the catalyst for Romania's entry into the war on Germany's side.

In 1940 Romania's territorial gains made following [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") were largely undone. In July, after a Soviet ultimatum, Romania agreed to [give up Bessarabia and northern Bukovina](/wiki/Soviet_occupation_of_Bessarabia_and_Northern_Bukovina "Soviet occupation of Bessarabia and Northern Bukovina") (the Soviets also annexed the city of [Hertsa](/wiki/Hertsa "Hertsa"), which was not stated in the ultimatum). Two-thirds of Bessarabia were combined with a [small part of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Moldavian_ASSR "Moldavian ASSR") to form the [Moldavian Soviet Socialist Republic](/wiki/Moldavian_SSR "Moldavian SSR"). The rest (northern Bukovina, the northern half of [Hotin county](/wiki/Hotin_county "Hotin county") and [Budjak](/wiki/Budjak "Budjak")) was apportioned to the [Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic](/wiki/Ukrainian_SSR "Ukrainian SSR").

Shortly thereafter, on 30 August, under the [Second Vienna Award](/wiki/Second_Vienna_Award "Second Vienna Award"), Germany and [Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy") mediated a compromise between Romania and the [Kingdom of Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary_(1920%E2%80%931946) "Kingdom of Hungary (1920–1946)"): Hungary received a region referred to as '[Northern Transylvania](/wiki/Northern_Transylvania "Northern Transylvania")', while 'Southern Transylvania' remained part of Romania (Hungary had lost [Transylvania](/wiki/Transylvania "Transylvania") after [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") in the [Treaty of Trianon](/wiki/Treaty_of_Trianon "Treaty of Trianon")). On 7 September, under the [Treaty of Craiova](/wiki/Treaty_of_Craiova "Treaty of Craiova"), [Southern Dobruja](/wiki/Southern_Dobruja "Southern Dobruja") (which Bulgaria had lost after the Romanian invasion during the [Second Balkan War](/wiki/Second_Balkan_War "Second Balkan War") in 1913), was ceded to Bulgaria under pressure from Germany. Despite the relatively recent acquisition of these territories, those were inhabited by a majority of Romanian speaking people (except Southern Dobruja), so the Romanians had seen them as historically belonging to Romania, and the fact that so much land was lost without a fight shattered the underpinnings of King Carol's power.

However, these cessions of territory to Hungary and Bulgaria did earn Romania a German guarantee of its remaining land. This guarantee was successfully put to test a few months later. On 13 November 1940, [Vyacheslav Molotov](/wiki/Vyacheslav_Molotov "Vyacheslav Molotov") asked Hitler to endorse the Soviet annexation of South Bukovina. This was equivalent to Germany cancelling its guarantee of Romania, something obviously unacceptable to Berlin. As the *Final Report* of the [Wiesel Commission](/wiki/Wiesel_Commission "Wiesel Commission") put it: "Only Hitler's refusal saved the rest of Bukovina from being swallowed up, Russified, and lost to Romania forever.".[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

On 4 July, [Ion Gigurtu](/wiki/Ion_Gigurtu "Ion Gigurtu") formed the first Romanian government to include an [Iron Guardist](/wiki/Iron_Guard "Iron Guard") minister, [Horia Sima](/wiki/Horia_Sima "Horia Sima"). Sima was a particularly virulent [antisemite](/wiki/Antisemitism "Antisemitism") who had become the nominal leader of the movement after the death of [Corneliu Codreanu](/wiki/Corneliu_Codreanu "Corneliu Codreanu"). He was one of the few prominent far-right leaders to survive the bloody infighting and government suppression of the preceding years.

## Antonescu comes to power

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_in_World_War_II&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Antonescu comes to power")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-B03212%2C_M%C3%BCnchen%2C_Staatsbesuch_Ion_Antonescu_bei_Hitler.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-B03212%2C_M%C3%BCnchen%2C_Staatsbesuch_Ion_Antonescu_bei_Hitler.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-B03212,_M%C3%BCnchen,_Staatsbesuch_Ion_Antonescu_bei_Hitler.jpg)

[Antonescu](/wiki/Ion_Antonescu "Ion Antonescu") and [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") at the *[Führerbau](/wiki/Hochschule_f%C3%BCr_Musik_und_Theater_M%C3%BCnchen "Hochschule für Musik und Theater München")* in [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich") (June 1941).

In the immediate wake of the loss of Northern Transylvania, on 4 September 1940, the Iron Guard (led by Horia Sima) and General (later Marshal) [Ion Antonescu](/wiki/Ion_Antonescu "Ion Antonescu") united to form the "[National Legionary State](/wiki/National_Legionary_State "National Legionary State")", which forced the abdication of Carol II in favor of his 19-year-old son [Michael](/wiki/Michael_of_Romania "Michael of Romania"). Carol and his mistress [Magda Lupescu](/wiki/Magda_Lupescu "Magda Lupescu") went into exile, and Romania, despite the unfavorable outcome of recent territorial disputes, leaned strongly toward the [Axis](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers"). As part of the deal, the Iron Guard became the sole legal party in Romania. Antonescu became the Iron Guard's honorary leader, while Sima became deputy premier.

In power, the Iron Guard stiffened the already harsh anti-Semitic legislation, enacted legislation directed against minority businessmen, tempered at times by the willingness of officials to take bribes, and wreaked vengeance upon its enemies. On 8 October 1940 German troops began crossing into Romania. They soon numbered over 500,000.

On 23 November Romania joined the Axis powers. On 27 November 1940, 64 former dignitaries or officials were executed by the [Iron Guard](/wiki/Iron_Guard "Iron Guard") in the [Jilava](/wiki/Jilava "Jilava") prison while awaiting trial (see *[Jilava Massacre](/wiki/Jilava_Massacre "Jilava Massacre")*). Later that day, historian and former prime minister [Nicolae Iorga](/wiki/Nicolae_Iorga "Nicolae Iorga") and economist [Virgil Madgearu](/wiki/Virgil_Madgearu "Virgil Madgearu"), a former government minister, were assassinated.

The cohabitation between the Iron Guard and Antonescu was never an easy one. On 20 January 1941, the Iron Guard attempted a [coup, combined with a bloody pogrom against the Jews](/wiki/Legionnaires%27_rebellion_and_Bucharest_pogrom "Legionnaires' rebellion and Bucharest pogrom") of [Bucharest](/wiki/Bucharest "Bucharest"). Within four days, Antonescu had successfully suppressed the coup. The Iron Guard was forced out of the government. Sima and many other legionnaires took refuge in Germany;[[9]](#cite_note-9) others were imprisoned. Antonescu abolished the National Legionary State, in its stead declaring Romania a "National and Social State."

## The war on the Eastern Front

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_in_World_War_II&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: The war on the Eastern Front")]

Further information: [Romanian armies in the Battle of Stalingrad](/wiki/Romanian_armies_in_the_Battle_of_Stalingrad "Romanian armies in the Battle of Stalingrad") and [Romanian Navy during World War II](/wiki/Romanian_Navy_during_World_War_II "Romanian Navy during World War II")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Ethnic_map_Romania_1941.png/250px-Ethnic_map_Romania_1941.png)](/wiki/File:Ethnic_map_Romania_1941.png)

Romania [annexed Transnistria](/wiki/Transnistria_Governorate "Transnistria Governorate"), the area between the [Dniester](/wiki/Dniester "Dniester") and [Southern Bug](/wiki/Southern_Bug "Southern Bug"), in July 1941 ([1941 Romanian census](/wiki/1941_Romanian_census "1941 Romanian census")).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/Razboiul_Sfant_Contra_Bolsevismului_%281941_stamp%29.svg/250px-Razboiul_Sfant_Contra_Bolsevismului_%281941_stamp%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Razboiul_Sfant_Contra_Bolsevismului_(1941_stamp).svg)

1941 stamp depicting a Romanian and a German soldier in reference to the two countries' common participation in [Operation Barbarossa](/wiki/Operation_Barbarossa "Operation Barbarossa"). The text below reads *"the holy war against [Bolshevism](/wiki/Bolshevism "Bolshevism")"*.

On 22 June 1941, German armies with a massive Romanian support attacked the Soviet Union. German and Romanian units conquered Bessarabia, Odessa, and Sevastopol, then marched eastward across the Russian steppes toward Stalingrad. Romania welcomed the war because it allowed them to retake lands annexed by the Soviet Union a year prior. Hitler rewarded Romania's loyalty by returning Bessarabia and northern Bukovina and by allowing Romania to administer Soviet lands immediately between the Dniester and the Bug, including Odessa and Nikolaev.[[10]](#cite_note-solonari-10) Romanian jingoes in Odessa even distributed a geography showing that the Dacians had inhabited most of southern Russia.[[1]](#cite_note-Study-1)[[11]](#cite_note-11) After recovering Bessarabia and Bukovina ([Operation München](/wiki/Operation_M%C3%BCnchen "Operation München")), Romanian units fought side by side with the Germans onward to [Odessa](/wiki/Odessa "Odessa"), [Sevastopol](/wiki/Sevastopol "Sevastopol"), [Stalingrad](/wiki/Stalingrad "Stalingrad") and the [Caucasus](/wiki/Caucasus "Caucasus"). The total number of troops involved on the Eastern Front with the [Romanian Third Army](/wiki/Third_Army_(Romania) "Third Army (Romania)") and the [Romanian Fourth Army](/wiki/Fourth_Army_(Romania) "Fourth Army (Romania)") was second only to that of Nazi Germany itself. The Romanian Army had a total of 686,258 men under arms in the summer of 1941 and a total of 1,224,691 men in the summer of 1944.[[12]](#cite_note-Sources-12) The number of Romanian troops sent to fight in the Soviet Union exceeded that of all of Germany's other allies combined. A *[Country Study](/wiki/Library_of_Congress_Country_Studies "Library of Congress Country Studies")* by the U.S. Federal Research Division of the Library of Congress attributes this to a "morbid competition with Hungary to curry Hitler's favor... [in hope of]... regaining northern Transylvania."[[1]](#cite_note-Study-1)

[Bessarabia](/wiki/Bessarabia "Bessarabia") and [Northern Bukovina](/wiki/Northern_Bukovina "Northern Bukovina") were now fully re-incorporated into the Romanian state after they had been [occupied by the USSR a year earlier](/wiki/Soviet_occupation_of_Bessarabia_and_Northern_Bukovina "Soviet occupation of Bessarabia and Northern Bukovina"). As a substitute for Northern Transylvania, which had been given to Hungary following the [Second Vienna Award](/wiki/Second_Vienna_Award "Second Vienna Award"), Hitler persuaded Antonescu in August 1941 to also take control of the [Transnistria](/wiki/Transnistria "Transnistria") territory between the [Dniester](/wiki/Dniester "Dniester") and the [Southern Bug](/wiki/Southern_Bug "Southern Bug"), which would also include [Odessa](/wiki/Odessa "Odessa") after its eventual [fall in October 1941](/wiki/Battle_of_Odessa_(1941) "Battle of Odessa (1941)"). Although the Romanian administration set up a civil government, the [Transnistria Governorate](/wiki/Transnistria_Governorate "Transnistria Governorate"), the Romanian state had not yet formally incorporated Transnistria into its administrative framework by the time it was retaken by Soviet troops in early 1944.

Romanian armies advanced far into the Soviet Union during 1941 and 1942 before being involved in the disaster at the [Battle of Stalingrad](/wiki/Battle_of_Stalingrad "Battle of Stalingrad") in the winter of 1942–43. [Petre Dumitrescu](/wiki/Petre_Dumitrescu "Petre Dumitrescu"), one of Romania's most important generals, was commander of the Third Army at Stalingrad. In November 1942, the [German Sixth Army](/wiki/6th_Army_(Wehrmacht) "6th Army (Wehrmacht)") was briefly put at Dumitrescu's disposal during a German attempt to relieve the Third Army following the devastating Soviet [Operation Uranus](/wiki/Operation_Uranus "Operation Uranus").

Prior to the Soviet counteroffensive at Stalingrad, the Antonescu government considered a war with Hungary over Transylvania an inevitability after the expected victory over the Soviet Union.[[1]](#cite_note-Study-1) Although it was an ally of Germany, Romania's later turning to the Allied side in August 1944 was rewarded by returning Northern Transylvania, which had been granted to Hungary in 1940 after the [Second Vienna Award](/wiki/Second_Vienna_Award "Second Vienna Award").

## War comes to Romania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_in_World_War_II&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: War comes to Romania")]

### Air raids

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_in_World_War_II&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Air raids")]

Main articles: [Bombing of Romania in World War II](/wiki/Bombing_of_Romania_in_World_War_II "Bombing of Romania in World War II") and [Western Allied campaign in Romania](/wiki/Western_Allied_campaign_in_Romania "Western Allied campaign in Romania")

See also: [Oil campaign of World War II](/wiki/Oil_campaign_of_World_War_II "Oil campaign of World War II")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/Operation_Tidal_Wave_in_1943.jpg/250px-Operation_Tidal_Wave_in_1943.jpg)](/wiki/File:Operation_Tidal_Wave_in_1943.jpg)

American [B-24 Liberator](/wiki/B-24_Liberator "B-24 Liberator") flying over a burning oil refinery at Ploiești, as part of [Operation Tidal Wave](/wiki/Operation_Tidal_Wave "Operation Tidal Wave") on 1 August 1943. Due to its role as a major supplier of oil to the Axis, Romania was a prime target of Allied strategic bombing in 1943 and 1944.

Throughout the Antonescu years, Romania supplied Nazi Germany and the Axis armies with oil, grain, and industrial products.[[1]](#cite_note-Study-1) Also, numerous train stations in the country, such as [Gara de Nord](/wiki/Gara_de_Nord "Gara de Nord") in Bucharest, served as transit points for troops departing for the Eastern Front. Consequently, by 1943 Romania became a target of Allied [aerial bombardment](/wiki/Strategic_bombing "Strategic bombing"). One of the most notable air bombardments was [Operation Tidal Wave](/wiki/Operation_Tidal_Wave "Operation Tidal Wave") — the attack on the [oil fields](/wiki/Oil_field "Oil field") of [Ploiești](/wiki/Ploie%C8%99ti "Ploiești") on 1 August 1943. Bucharest was subjected to intense Allied bombardment on 4 and 15 April 1944, and the [Luftwaffe](/wiki/Luftwaffe "Luftwaffe") itself bombed the city on 24 and 25 August after the country switched sides.

### Ground offensive

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_in_World_War_II&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Ground offensive")]

In February 1943, with the decisive [Soviet counteroffensive at Stalingrad](/wiki/Battle_of_Stalingrad "Battle of Stalingrad"), it was growing clear that the tide of the war was turning against the [Axis powers](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers").

By 1944, the Romanian economy was in tatters because of the expenses of the war, and destructive [Allied air bombing throughout the country](/wiki/Bombing_of_Romania_in_World_War_II "Bombing of Romania in World War II"), including the capital, [Bucharest](/wiki/Bombing_of_Bucharest_in_World_War_II "Bombing of Bucharest in World War II"). In addition, most of the products sent to Germany – such as oil, grain, and equipment – were provided without monetary compensation, as [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") refused to pay. As a result of these uncompensated exports, [inflation](/wiki/Inflation "Inflation") in Romania skyrocketed. This caused widespread discontent among the Romanian population, even among those who had once enthusiastically supported the Germans and the war, and an angry relationship between Romania and Germany.[[1]](#cite_note-Study-1)

Beginning in December 1943, the Soviet [Dnieper–Carpathian Offensive](/wiki/Dnieper%E2%80%93Carpathian_Offensive "Dnieper–Carpathian Offensive") pushed Axis forces all the way back to the Dniester by April 1944. In April–May 1944, the Romanian forces led by General [Mihai Racoviță](/wiki/Ioan_Mihail_Racovi%C8%9B%C4%83 "Ioan Mihail Racoviță"), together with elements of the [German Eighth Army](/wiki/8th_Army_(Wehrmacht) "8th Army (Wehrmacht)") were responsible for defending northern Romania and took part in the [Battles of Târgu Frumos](/wiki/First_Battle_of_T%C3%A2rgu_Frumos "First Battle of Târgu Frumos"), which [David Glantz](/wiki/David_Glantz "David Glantz") considered to be an [initial Soviet attempt to invade Romania](/wiki/First_Jassy%E2%80%93Kishinev_Offensive "First Jassy–Kishinev Offensive"), supposedly held back by Axis defensive lines in northern Romania.

The [Jassy–Kishinev Offensive](/wiki/Jassy%E2%80%93Kishinev_Offensive_(August_1944) "Jassy–Kishinev Offensive (August 1944)"), launched on 20 August 1944, resulted in a quick and decisive Soviet breakthrough, collapsing the German-Romanian front in the region. Soviet forces captured [Târgu Frumos](/wiki/T%C3%A2rgu_Frumos "Târgu Frumos") and [Iași](/wiki/Ia%C8%99i "Iași") on 21 August and [Chișinău](/wiki/Chi%C8%99in%C4%83u "Chișinău") on 24 August 1944.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The strategic [Focșani Gate](/wiki/Foc%C8%99ani_Gate "Focșani Gate") was invaded on 27 August 1944 by Soviet forces, which allowed them to spread out onto Bucharest, the Black Sea and the Eastern Carpathians.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## The Holocaust

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_in_World_War_II&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: The Holocaust")]

Main article: [The Holocaust in Romania](/wiki/The_Holocaust_in_Romania "The Holocaust in Romania")

:   *See also [Responsibility for the Holocaust (Romania)](/wiki/Responsibility_for_the_Holocaust#Romania "Responsibility for the Holocaust"), [Antonescu and the Holocaust](/wiki/Ion_Antonescu#Antonescu_and_the_Holocaust "Ion Antonescu")*, *[Porajmos#Persecution in other Axis countries](/wiki/Porajmos#Persecution_in_other_Axis_countries "Porajmos")*.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Templul_evreilor_spanioli_din_Bucure%C5%9Fti.jpg/250px-Templul_evreilor_spanioli_din_Bucure%C5%9Fti.jpg)](/wiki/File:Templul_evreilor_spanioli_din_Bucure%C5%9Fti.jpg)

[Sephardic](/wiki/Sephardic "Sephardic") Temple in [Bucharest](/wiki/Bucharest "Bucharest") after it was plundered and set on fire in 1941

According to an [international commission report](/wiki/Wiesel_Commission "Wiesel Commission") released by the Romanian government in 2004, between 280,000 and 380,000 Jews were murdered or died in various forms on Romanian soil, in the war zones of [Bessarabia](/wiki/Bessarabia "Bessarabia"), [Bukovina](/wiki/Bukovina "Bukovina"), and in the Soviet territories under Romanian occupation ([Transnistria Governorate](/wiki/Transnistria_Governorate "Transnistria Governorate")). Of the 25,000 [Romani](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people") deported, who were deported to concentration camps in Transnistria, 11,000 died.[[14]](#cite_note-Commission-14)

Though much of the killing was committed in the war zone by Romanian and German troops, there were also substantial persecutions behind the front line. During the [Iaşi pogrom](/wiki/Ia%C5%9Fi_pogrom "Iaşi pogrom") of June 1941, over 13,000 Jews were massacred or killed slowly in trains traveling back and forth across the countryside.

Half of the estimated 270,000 to 320,000 Jews living in Bessarabia, Bukovina, and [Dorohoi County](/wiki/Dorohoi_County "Dorohoi County") in Romania were murdered or died between June 1941 and the spring of 1944, of which between 45,000 and 60,000 Jews were killed in Bessarabia and Bukovina by Romanian and German troops, within months from the entry of the country into the war during 1941. Even after the initial killings, Jews in [Moldavia](/wiki/Western_Moldavia "Western Moldavia"), Bukovina and Bessarabia were subject to frequent [pogroms](/wiki/Pogroms "Pogroms"), and were concentrated into [ghettos](/wiki/Ghettos "Ghettos") from which they were sent to Transnistria, including camps built and run by the Romanian authorities.

Romanian soldiers and gendarmes also worked with the *[Einsatzkommandos](/wiki/Einsatzkommando "Einsatzkommando")*, German killing squads, tasked with massacring Jews and Roma in conquered territories, the local Ukrainian militia, and the SS squads of local Ukrainian Germans ([Sonderkommando Russland](/wiki/Volksdeutsche_Mittelstelle#Role_in_the_Holocaust "Volksdeutsche Mittelstelle") and [Selbstschutz](/wiki/Selbstschutz "Selbstschutz")). Romanian troops were in large part responsible for the [Odessa massacre](/wiki/1941_Odessa_massacre "1941 Odessa massacre"), in which from October 18, 1941, until mid-March 1942, Romanian soldiers in Odessa, aided by gendarmes and police, killed up to 25,000 Jews and deported more than 35,000.[[14]](#cite_note-Commission-14)

The number of deaths in all areas is not certain, but the lowest respectable estimates run to about 250,000 Jews and 11,000 Roma in these eastern regions.

Nonetheless, half of the Jews living within the pre-Barbarossa borders survived the war, although they were subject to a wide range of harsh conditions, including forced labor, financial penalties, and discriminatory laws. All Jewish property was [nationalized](/wiki/Nationalization "Nationalization").

The report commissioned and accepted by the Romanian government in 2004 on the Holocaust concluded:[[14]](#cite_note-Commission-14)

> *Of all the allies of Nazi Germany, Romania bears responsibility for the deaths of more Jews than any country other than Germany itself. The murders committed in [Iasi](/wiki/Ia%C8%99i_pogrom "Iași pogrom"), [Odessa](/wiki/1941_Odessa_massacre "1941 Odessa massacre"), [Bogdanovka](/wiki/Bogdanovka_concentration_camp "Bogdanovka concentration camp"), Domanovka, and Peciora, for example, were among the most hideous murders committed against Jews anywhere during the Holocaust. Romania committed [genocide](/wiki/Genocide "Genocide") against the Jews. The survival of Jews in some parts of the country does not alter this reality.*

## The royal coup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_in_World_War_II&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: The royal coup")]

Main article: [King Michael's Coup](/wiki/King_Michael%27s_Coup "King Michael's Coup")

See also: [Operation Margarethe II](/wiki/Operation_Margarethe_II "Operation Margarethe II")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Mihai.jpg/250px-Mihai.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mihai.jpg)

King [Michael I of Romania](/wiki/Michael_I_of_Romania "Michael I of Romania") led the coup that put Romania on the Allied side.

On 23 August 1944, with the Red Army penetrating German defenses during the [Jassy–Kishinev Offensive](/wiki/Second_Jassy%E2%80%93Kishinev_Offensive "Second Jassy–Kishinev Offensive"), King [Michael I of Romania](/wiki/Michael_I_of_Romania "Michael I of Romania") led a successful coup against the Axis with support from opposition politicians, most of the army and [Communist](/wiki/Romanian_Communist_Party "Romanian Communist Party")-led civilians.[[15]](#cite_note-Library-15) Michael I, who was initially considered to be not much more than a figurehead, was able to successfully depose the Antonescu dictatorship. The King then offered a non-confrontational retreat to German ambassador [Manfred von Killinger](/wiki/Manfred_von_Killinger "Manfred von Killinger"). But the Germans considered the coup "reversible" and attempted to turn the situation around by military force. The Romanian [First](/wiki/First_Army_(Romania) "First Army (Romania)"), [Second (forming)](/wiki/Second_Army_(Romania) "Second Army (Romania)"), and what little was left of the [Third](/wiki/Third_Army_(Romania) "Third Army (Romania)") and the [Fourth Armies](/wiki/Fourth_Army_(Romania) "Fourth Army (Romania)") (one corps) were under orders from the King to defend Romania against any German attacks. King Michael offered to put the Romanian Army, which at that point had a strength of nearly 1,000,000 men,[[16]](#cite_note-16) on the side of the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II"). Stalin immediately recognized the king and the restoration of the conservative Romanian monarchy.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

In a radio broadcast to the Romanian nation and army on the night of 23 August King Michael issued a cease-fire,[[15]](#cite_note-Library-15) proclaimed Romania's loyalty to the Allies, announced the acceptance of an armistice (to be signed on September 12)[[18]](#cite_note-18) offered by Great Britain, the United States, and the [USSR](/wiki/USSR "USSR"), and declared war on Germany.[[19]](#cite_note-19) The coup accelerated the [Red Army](/wiki/Red_Army "Red Army")'s advance into Romania, but did not avert a rapid Soviet occupation and capture of about 130,000 Romanian soldiers, who were transported to the Soviet Union, where many perished in prison camps. The armistice was signed three weeks later on September 12, 1944, on terms virtually dictated by the Soviet Union.[[15]](#cite_note-Library-15) Under the terms of the armistice, Romania announced its unconditional surrender[[20]](#cite_note-20) to the USSR and was placed under occupation of the Allied forces with the Soviet Union as their representative, in control of media, communication, post, and civil administration behind the front.[[15]](#cite_note-Library-15) Some attribute the postponement of a formal Allied recognition of the *de facto* change of orientation until 12 September (the date the armistice was signed in Moscow) to the complexities of the negotiations between the USSR and UK.[[21]](#cite_note-Honest-21)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/1944soviet1.png/250px-1944soviet1.png)](/wiki/File:1944soviet1.png)

[Nicolae Ceaușescu](/wiki/Nicolae_Ceau%C8%99escu "Nicolae Ceaușescu") and others welcome the Red Army as it enters Bucharest on 30 August 1944

During the [Moscow Conference](/wiki/Moscow_Conference_(1944) "Moscow Conference (1944)") in October 1944 [Winston Churchill](/wiki/Winston_Churchill "Winston Churchill"), [Prime Minister of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Prime Minister of the United Kingdom"), proposed an [agreement](/wiki/Percentages_agreement "Percentages agreement") to [Soviet leader](/wiki/General_Secretary_of_the_Communist_Party_of_the_Soviet_Union "General Secretary of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union") [Joseph Stalin](/wiki/Joseph_Stalin "Joseph Stalin") on how to split up [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe") into spheres of influence after the war. The Soviet Union was offered a 90% share of influence in Romania.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

The Armistice Agreement of 12 September stipulated in Article 18 that "An Allied Control Commission will be established which will undertake until the conclusion of peace the regulation of and control over the execution of the present terms under the general direction and orders of the Allied (Soviet) High Command, acting on behalf of the Allied Powers". The Annex to Article 18 made clear that "The Romanian Government and their organs shall fulfil all instructions of the Allied Control Commission arising out of the Armistice Agreement." The Agreement also stipulated that the [Allied Control Commission](/wiki/Allied_Control_Commission#Romania "Allied Control Commission") would have its seat in [Bucharest](/wiki/Bucharest "Bucharest"). In line with Article 14 of the Armistice Agreement, two [Romanian People's Tribunals](/wiki/Romanian_People%27s_Tribunals "Romanian People's Tribunals") were set up to try suspected war criminals.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

## Campaign against the Axis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_in_World_War_II&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Campaign against the Axis")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/WWII_Southern_Central_Europe_1944-1945.png/250px-WWII_Southern_Central_Europe_1944-1945.png)](/wiki/File:WWII_Southern_Central_Europe_1944-1945.png)

Romanian operations against the [Axis](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Romanian_soldiers_in_Transilvania_1944.jpg/250px-Romanian_soldiers_in_Transilvania_1944.jpg)](/wiki/File:Romanian_soldiers_in_Transilvania_1944.jpg)

Romanian soldiers in [Transylvania](/wiki/Transylvania "Transylvania"), September–October 1944

As the country declared war on Germany on the night of 23 August 1944, border clashes between Hungarian and Romanian troops erupted almost immediately. On 24 August, German troops attempted to seize Bucharest and suppress Michael's coup, but were repelled by the city's defenses. Other Wehrmacht units in the country suffered severe losses: remnants of the Sixth Army retreating west of the [Prut River](/wiki/Prut "Prut") were cut off and destroyed by the Red Army, which was now advancing at an even greater speed, while Romanian units attacked German garrisons at the [Ploiești](/wiki/Ploie%C8%99ti "Ploiești") oilfields, forcing them to retreat to Hungary. The Romanian Army captured over 50,000 German prisoners around this time, who were later surrendered to the Soviets.[[24]](#cite_note-jn-24)

In early September 1944, Soviet and Romanian forces entered Transylvania and captured the towns of [Brașov](/wiki/Bra%C8%99ov "Brașov") and [Sibiu](/wiki/Sibiu "Sibiu") while advancing toward the [Mureș River](/wiki/Mure%C8%99_River "Mureș River"). Their main objective was [Cluj](/wiki/Cluj-Napoca "Cluj-Napoca"), a city regarded as the historical capital of Transylvania. However, the [Second Hungarian Army](/wiki/Second_Army_(Hungary) "Second Army (Hungary)") was present in the region, and together with the [Eighth German Army](/wiki/8th_Army_(Wehrmacht) "8th Army (Wehrmacht)") engaged the Allied forces on 5 September 1944 in what was to become the [Battle of Turda](/wiki/Battle_of_Turda "Battle of Turda"), which lasted until 8 October and resulted in heavy casualties for both sides.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Also around this time, the Hungarian Army carried out its last independent offensive action of the war, penetrating [Arad County](/wiki/Arad_County "Arad County") in western Romania. Despite initial success, a number of ad-hoc Romanian cadet battalions managed to stop the Hungarian advance at the [Battle of Păuliș](/wiki/Battle_of_P%C4%83uli%C8%99 "Battle of Păuliș"), and soon a combined Romanian-Soviet counterattack overwhelmed the Hungarians, who gave ground and evacuated [Arad](/wiki/Arad,_Romania "Arad, Romania") itself on 21 September.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [Battle of Carei](/wiki/Battle_of_Carei "Battle of Carei") marked the last stage of recovering Romania's former territory of [Northern Transylvania](/wiki/Northern_Transylvania "Northern Transylvania"), ceded in 1940 to Hungary as a result of the [Second Vienna Award](/wiki/Second_Vienna_Award "Second Vienna Award"). On the evening of October 24, 1944, the Romanian 6th Army Corps attacked in the direction of [Carei](/wiki/Carei "Carei") with a force comprising 4 divisions; at the same time, the 2nd Infantry Division of 2nd Army Corps attacked in the direction of [Satu Mare](/wiki/Satu_Mare "Satu Mare"), in a pincer movement. On October 25, both cities were freed from [Hungarian](/wiki/Government_of_National_Unity_(Hungary) "Government of National Unity (Hungary)") and [German](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") control; by a decree from 1959, this day was established as the [Romanian Armed Forces Day](/wiki/Armed_Forces_Day#Romania "Armed Forces Day").[[25]](#cite_note-defense-25)[[26]](#cite_note-Ilie-26)

The [Romanian Army](/wiki/Romanian_Army "Romanian Army") ended the war fighting against the [Wehrmacht](/wiki/Wehrmacht "Wehrmacht") alongside the Red Army in Transylvania, Hungary, [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Yugoslavia "Yugoslavia"), Austria and the [Protectorate of Bohemia and Moravia](/wiki/Protectorate_of_Bohemia_and_Moravia "Protectorate of Bohemia and Moravia"), from August 1944 until the [end of the war in Europe](/wiki/End_of_World_War_II_in_Europe "End of World War II in Europe"). In May 1945, the [First](/wiki/Romanian_First_Army "Romanian First Army") and [Fourth](/wiki/Romanian_Fourth_Army "Romanian Fourth Army") armies took part in the [Prague Offensive](/wiki/Prague_Offensive "Prague Offensive"). The Romanian Army incurred heavy casualties fighting Nazi Germany. Of some 538,000 Romanian soldiers who fought against the Axis in 1944–45, some 167,000 were killed, wounded or went missing.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

## Aftermath

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romania_in_World_War_II&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Aftermath")]

Main articles: [Soviet occupation of Bessarabia and Northern Bukovina](/wiki/Soviet_occupation_of_Bessarabia_and_Northern_Bukovina "Soviet occupation of Bessarabia and Northern Bukovina"), [Soviet occupation of Romania](/wiki/Soviet_occupation_of_Romania "Soviet occupation of Romania"), and [Socialist Republic of Romania](/wiki/Socialist_Republic_of_Romania "Socialist Republic of Romania")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Romania_WWII.png/250px-Romania_WWII.png)](/wiki/File:Romania_WWII.png)

Map of Romania after World War II indicating lost territories.

Under the [1947 Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Paris_Peace_Treaties,_1947 "Paris Peace Treaties, 1947"),[[31]](#cite_note-31) the Allies did not acknowledge Romania as a co-belligerent nation but instead applied the term "ally of Hitlerite Germany" to all recipients of the treaty's stipulations. Like Finland, Romania had to pay $300 million to the Soviet Union as war reparations. However, the treaty specifically recognized that Romania switched sides on 24 August 1944, and therefore "acted in the interests of all the United Nations". As a reward, Northern Transylvania was, once again, recognized as an integral part of Romania, but the border with the USSR and Bulgaria was fixed at its state in January 1941, restoring the pre-Barbarossa status quo (with [one exception](/wiki/T%C4%83taru_Mare_Island "Tătaru Mare Island")). Following the [dissolution of the Soviet Union](/wiki/History_of_the_Soviet_Union_(1985%E2%80%931991) "History of the Soviet Union (1985–1991)") in 1991, the Eastern territories became part of Ukraine and the [Republic of Moldova](/wiki/Moldova "Moldova").

In Romania proper, [Soviet occupation](/wiki/Soviet_occupation_of_Romania "Soviet occupation of Romania") following World War II facilitated the rise of the [Communist Party](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Romania "Communist Party of Romania") as the main political force, leading ultimately to the abdication of the King and the establishment of a single-party [people's republic](/wiki/Socialist_Republic_of_Romania "Socialist Republic of Romania") in 1947.
